13. Pour water over it to remove any adhering earth particles. Care should be
taken not to brush it to prevent obliteration of the details of the footprint.

Modern Methods of Collection

Electrostatic technique

The dust particles adhering to the foot or foot wear that form a dusty
print on the floor can be lifted by the electrostatic technique. A black vinyl
sheet is placed over the suspected area containing the foot or foot wear print
and it is covered with aluminium foil through which a high voltage of current
(1500 volts) is passed. The dust particles that form the foot print are
attracted towards the vinyl sheet and become attached to it and can be
photographed for a permanent record.

94

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\HLP of Physical Evidence & Questionnaire in LOA (CIVIL).pdf
metadata.filename: HLP of Physical Evidence & Questionnaire in LOA (CIVIL).pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures

page_content:
Holography (laser technique)

This technique was developed by Dr. Bradford, a British scientist
which involves the use of laser beams to locate the foot prints or shoe prints
on the floor carpet. When a person walks on a carpet, the fibers of the
carpet are compressed on account of the pressure exerted by the weight of
the person and a contour of foot or footwear is left on the carpet. With laser
beams it is possible to record such compressed marks on a photographic
plate. This technique is known as holography and such a photograph is
known as a hologram.

Collection of Test Prints for Comparison

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\HLP of Physical Evidence & Questionnaire in LOA (CIVIL).pdf
metadata.filename: HLP of Physical Evidence & Questionnaire in LOA (CIVIL).pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures

page_content:
Collection of Test Prints for Comparison

The crime scene foot prints have to be compared with the test print
obtained under similar conditions from the suspect/ accused. e.g., if the
surface crime print is made with a liquid medium, the test print must also
be made with a liquid medium for comparison. If the surface crime print is
in human blood the test print should be caused with goat or fowl blood. To
get the complete picture of the sole it is worthwhile to obtain prints in
different positions i.e., in normal standing position, with pressures on inner
and outer side, when walking, running etc.

Precautions in Collection of Foot Prints and Footwear Prints

1. If the footprints are on easily removable articles, they should be packed
separately and forwarded.

2. If the prints cannot be removed immediately or recorded they should be
covered with inverted hollow cardboard boxes or any other suitable
objects.

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\HLP of Physical Evidence & Questionnaire in LOA (CIVIL).pdf
metadata.filename: HLP of Physical Evidence & Questionnaire in LOA (CIVIL).pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures

page_content:
3. If footprints are in open places, adequate precautions should be taken for
protection.

4. All prints and impressions should be photographed before attempting
any other method.

5. While taking photographs:
e The lens of the camera should be kept parallel to the surface on which
the print is found.

e Ascale or foot rule is to be kept along the length of the footprint.

e A paper containing identification details such as date, crime number
police station, location, left/right foot print etc., should be kept besides
the footprint.

6. Incase of tracing of footprints:
e The sheet of glass or celluloid should not actually touch the impression.
e The sheet should not be moved until the tracing is complete.

95

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\HLP of Physical Evidence & Questionnaire in LOA (CIVIL).pdf
metadata.filename: HLP of Physical Evidence & Questionnaire in LOA (CIVIL).pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures

page_content:
e The lines should be drawn as thin as possible.

e The part of the foot for which the tracing is to be made, the tip of the pen
and the eye should be in a line vertically above the print.

e The clear edge of the different parts of the footprint should be shown in
thin continuous lines.

e The faint and doubtful impressions may be shown with dotted lines.
F. Spurious Articles
Control sample of same size, batch, manufacturing date has to be

procured from authorised dealer/manufacturer along with certificate as
that of the suspected article.

G. Dust and Dirt

1. Dirt and the specimens are removed from garments by several methods.
Large quantities are removed by the usual mechanical means of

15. BALLISTICS

Ballistics is the study of projectiles in motion. The term ballistics is
derived from the Latin word “ballista” which refers to a crossbow like device
for throwing stones by means of twisted ropes. Forensic Ballistics is the
examination and identification of firearm, cartridges, fired cartridges, fired
cartridge cases, bullets, gun powder, wads, pellets, etc. Further ballistics
examiner links the firearm with fired cartridge cases and bullets by
comparison of firing pin markings and rifling markings and also determines
the range of fire & angle of firing etc.

Laws of cases under which Ballistic Evidence are collected:
Arms Act, encounter, 302 IPC, 307 IPC, accidental firing etc.,

Handling and Lifting

1.

When lifting a pistol or revolver the best way is to hold it with two fingers
on the butt, or by the ring of the butt.

Do not lift a firearm by placing a stick or similar object in the trigger
guard, as the firearm may be cocked.

Questioned Document

“Any document or object that contains a handwritten, typewritten or
printed marking, whose source or authenticity is in doubt or which is under
scrutiny is referred to as a questioned document”. Such a broad definition
covers all the written and printed materials that are normally encountered
in our daily, social and business activities.

Any controversy or claim over a document is called a disputed
document. Disputed part may be _ initial/signature/writing or any
tampering.

Precautions and guidelines for collection of standards
Standards

Hand writings or signatures or initials etc., written by a known person
taken for the purpose of comparison with the questioned are known as
standards. Standards also include typed/printed scripts, seals and stamp
impressions etc., taken from a known source.

Standard Writings are of two types

e Admitted Writings
e Specimen Writings

Admitted writings

metadata.source: data\english\investigation_procedures\HLP of Physical Evidence & Questionnaire in LOA (CIVIL).pdf
metadata.filename: HLP of Physical Evidence & Questionnaire in LOA (CIVIL).pdf
metadata.subject: investigation_procedures

page_content:
Standard Writings are of two types

e Admitted Writings
e Specimen Writings

Admitted writings

Admitted writings are those written by a person in his/her routine
course of activity/business, which are freely written, natural and
undistorted. Such writings are normally available in case of employees,
students, working class people, professionals, business men etc. The most
suitable admitted standards are those which belong to the same general
class (post-card with post-card) and contemporary (near about
date/month/year) to the questioned documents. The investigating officer
should endeavor to collect standards, which would enable the expert to
compare “LIKE WITH LIKE” i.e., initials with initials, signatures with
signatures, writings with writings of the same language, cheques with
cheques and so on and so forth depending on the nature of the documents.

The admitted standard material can be procured in the following forms
of documents

The admitted standard material can be procured in the following forms
of documents

e Personal Correspondence: Post cards, inland letters, applications for
employment / loans / ration cards / passports / diaries / account
books, services like electricity, telephone, etc.

e Academic Records: School/College/applications, answer scripts, notes,
records, project reports etc.

Financial and Business Documents: Pass book, bank cheques,
applications for demand drafts, withdrawal forms, account opening
forms, specimen signature cards, ledgers, receipts etc.

Vocational & Professional Documents: Official correspondence, leave
letters, applications for loans, (P.F., LIC etc.,) attendance register, legal
documents, etc.

Miscellaneous Documents: Railway/bus reservation forms etc.

Specimen writings

Specimens are the writings specially written by the suspect for the
purpose of comparison with the disputed documents. This is usually done
either in the open court or in the presence of presiding officer. However the
specimen writing of the suspect can be taken only if he volunteers to give.
For collection of such specimens, there are some essential points to be
followed to facilitate expert’s examination meaningful, objective and
conclusive, as indicated below;

1. The suspect should be seated in a comfortable posture to ensure comfort

1. The suspect should be seated in a comfortable posture to ensure comfort

and natural writing conditions.

2. He/she should be supplied with similar paper and writing instruments

pen/ball pen) as that of the questioned.

3. The suspect should not be shown the questioned document

The dictation should be given to the suspect at different intervals to
prevent the suspect from imitating the same style.

5. After each dictation, the sheet of specimens should be removed, and a

resh sheet of paper to be supplied.

6. The suspect should be encouraged to write the word combination and

numerals as present in the questioned sample several times.

7. At least 20 specimen signatures / writings on different sheets should be

taken. In case of deliberate efforts to disguise, more specimens should be
taken.

8. The specimen writings/signature should be taken in the open court and

attested by the Judicial Officer/ Magistrate.

Marking

8. The specimen writings/signature should be taken in the open court and

attested by the Judicial Officer/ Magistrate.

Marking

The process of segregating the disputed portion of text from out of the
total document by marking distinctively for comparison is known as
‘Marking of documents’. The questioned portions should be encircled and
numbered as Q1, Q2, Q3....... respectively with red color pencil , whereas
the standard portions should be encircled and numbered as S1, S2, S3.....
respectively with blue color pencil.

Procedure for obtaining specimen handwriting

1. The suspect should be given similar paper as that of Question
Document.

2. The suspect should be given’ similar writing instrument.
Inform the style (Capitals / Small letters / language) in which the
suspect has to furnish his writings.

Question Document should not be shown to the suspect.

4. Allow the suspect to sit comfortably & dictate the writing at a reasonable

him. Similarly the second, third etc.,

6. Time taken to write each specimen, age of suspect, his physical condition

back side of the sheet by the I.O.

7. Specimens should be taken at different intervals.

18. FORENSIC SCIENCE LABORATORY AND ITS
STRUCTURE

1. The then Government of Andhra Pradesh established a full-fledged
Forensic Science Laboratory in the year 1974. The Laboratory was
headed by a Director who reports to the Director General of Police.

2. Four Joint Directors support the Director and are in-charge of
Biological, Chemical, Physical and General Divisions. Under each
division there are different sections, in total 12 sections. They are
Forensic Chemistry, Forensic Toxicology, Forensic Narcotics, Forensic
Serology, Forensic Biology, DNA Finger Printing, Forensic Physics,
Forensic Ballistics, Audio-Video Authentication, Forensic Computers,
Forensic Documents, and Forensic Psychology.

JD Biology - Biology, Serology, and DNA
JD Chemical - Toxicology, General Chemistry, Narcotics
JD General - Documents, Polygraph, Forensic Computers, &

Audio-Video authentication
JD Physical - Ballistics, Physics,

page_content:

JD Physical - Ballistics, Physics,

3. Each section is headed by an Assistant Director with supporting scientific
staff in the cadre of Scientific Officers, Scientific Assistants, Lab
Assistants, Lab Technicians and Lab Attendants. The Assistant Directors
are responsible for overall functioning of the 12 sections mentioned
above. In all scientific matters relating to physical evidence collection,
analysis, reports and their interpretation or for any clarification or advice
the concerned Assistant Directors are to be contacted by Investigating
Officers.

page_content:
4. There are three Regional Forensic Science Laboratories functioning in the
state of Telangana. They are Warangal, Kamareddy and Karimnagar. The
regional laboratories are headed by Assistant Directors with other
supporting scientific and ministerial staff; undertake examination of
physical evidence pertaining to toxicology, serology, chemistry and
biology sections only. These laboratories work under the direct
administrative control of Director, Forensic Science Laboratory.

Other Expert Institutions
1. Clues Team

The Mobile investigation units known as “CLUES” teams are
functioning in all Commissionerates and District Head Quarters for
processing the crime scene evidence, reconstruction of crime scene and for
providing assistance to the investigating officers at the crime scene. The

111

metadata.subject: investigation_procedures

page_content:
team consists of Scientific Expert, Fingerprint Expert, Photographer and
Videographer. The Scientific Experts will be the in-charge of this team.
These four members together constitute the CLUES team, which visits the
scenes of crime whenever their services are requisitioned. The CLUES teams
are under administration control of Director, Forensic Science Laboratory,
but their operational and functional control lies with concerned district
Superintendent of Police, Commissioner of Police in cities.

Time limit for sending physical evidence to APFSL for
scientific analysis

The investigating officers should collect and forward all types of
physical evidence along with controlled samples so as to reach the FSL
within 30 days from the date of crime. Physical evidence sent after 30 days
will not be accepted by FSL except in rare cases that too along with a D.O.
letter from the concerned SP/DCP addressed to the Director explaining the
reasons for the delay beyond 30 days.
